voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Worldwide — grasslands, meadows, and woodland edges
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its large ears can turn in different directions to pick up sounds.
Its eyes are placed on the sides of its head for a wide view.
It uses its nose constantly, sniffing for predators and familiar scents.
Long whiskers help it feel whether a burrow entrance is wide enough.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in meadows, fields, and woodland edges across many continents.
It digs a burrow system called a warren for sleeping and raising young.
Open ground nearby lets it graze on grass and low plants.
Soft soil is needed so it can dig and expand its tunnels.
How does it send messages?: It thumps its back feet hard on the ground to warn others of danger.
It rubs its chin on objects to leave a scent mark.
A white flash of its tail when it runs alerts other rabbits nearby.
It screams loudly only when badly frightened or injured.
What does it do when something changes?: It freezes when it first senses something unusual nearby.
Then it bolts in a zigzag path toward the nearest cover.
After a scare, it returns more cautiously and keeps nose up.
It can shift from daytime to nighttime grazing to avoid disturbance.
How does it learn?: Kits watch their mother and other rabbits to learn safe feeding spots.
Young rabbits follow adults before foraging on their own.
They learn danger signals from the group by watching who reacts first.
How does it have babies?: A female can give birth to five or six young at one time.
Newborns are hairless and blind and stay in a lined nest underground.
The mother visits the nest briefly at night to nurse the young.
Young rabbits grow quickly and leave the nest in about three weeks.
What are its limits?: Open ground with no cover forces it to stay near burrow entrances.
A loud sound or fast movement makes it freeze or sprint for safety.
Very cold winters limit its food and slow its activity.
It must drink water or eat moist plants to avoid dehydration.
What does this teach us?: A rabbit that seems calm may still be watching and smelling everything.
Its whole body is built for sensing trouble before it arrives.
Its choices about where to feed and rest are shaped by hidden risks.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Sensory system is weighted toward hearing, smell, and wide peripheral vision over central focus.
Lives in warren systems — networks of burrows, not single dens.
Young are altricial: born blind, hairless, and fully dependent on the nest.
Mother rabbits visit the nest briefly at night and otherwise leave young alone.
still_debated: How much social information is coded in chin-rubbing versus fecal pellet placement.
The role of the white-tail flash — alarm, pursuit deterrent, or both.
Exact population impact of modern predator shifts (domestic cats, etc.).
common_failure_modes: Applying domestic pet-rabbit behavior to wild rabbit biology.
Describing solitary behavior when wild rabbits are social within warrens.
Interpreting freezing as 'being scared' rather than as a hard-wired first response.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not confuse Oryctolagus cuniculus with hares (Lepus), jackrabbits, or cottontails.
Do not describe the mother abandoning young — brief visits are normal.
Do not say rabbits 'purr' — the sound is tooth-grinding, not a vocal purr.
